metadata.title: Quick-fire: Gift, loan, or no
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Fourth resource-allocation quick-fire. Where qf-001 varied counterparty over clean cash, qf-003 varied resource-shape, and qf-005 varied resource-type (time/expertise/network), this round varies the *structure* of help: gift (no expectation), loan (timelined return), recurring support (open-ended), or decline. Same allocation but different forms of obligation created.
metadata.domain_anchor: Sahlins 1972 on generalized vs balanced reciprocity; Cropanzano-Mitchell social-exchange theory for the gift-vs-loan distinction; Mauss 1925 The Gift on the obligations that gifts create.

items.prompt: A close friend asks for $500. It's not a crisis — they just want it for something that's hard to afford this month.

options.text: Give it as a gift; tell them not to worry about paying back.
Lend it with an explicit timeline (one month, three months). Let the structure protect the friendship.

items.prompt: A cousin is starting a small business and asks you to invest. They're not asking for charity — they want a real investment. The numbers are reasonable but it's their first venture.

options.text: Invest with structured terms (equity, repayment plan, named amount).
Decline. Mixing family and money at investment-level changes the relationship in ways you don't want.

items.prompt: A community member you don't know personally is fundraising for mutual aid through a community channel. The ask is real, the amount is small to you.

options.text: Contribute. Mutual-aid contexts rely on people who can give doing so without overthinking it.
Pass. Without knowing them or the situation directly, it's hard to know what you're contributing to.

items.prompt: A coworker mentions in passing that they've been having a tight financial month. They didn't ask for help. You could offer something — a meal, a small loan, picking up their share at lunches.

options.text: Offer something specific and small ('Hey, lunch is on me this week'). Concrete generosity without obligation.
Don't volunteer. They didn't ask; offering risks reading as condescending or making them feel obligated.

items.prompt: A friend whose work has substantially benefited you (creative work you've enjoyed for years, advice that helped your career) puts up an anonymous tip-jar/donation page for their work. You don't normally tip-jar.

options.text: Contribute a meaningful amount — what their work has been worth to you, not what the platform suggests.
Send the platform-suggested small amount. The friendship is the reciprocation; the money is symbolic.

items.prompt: An adult child / younger family member is buying their first home. You could help with a portion of the down payment.

options.text: Give it as a no-strings gift. They're family; that's what the resources are for.
Match a portion of what they save themselves over the next year. Tied to their own contribution rather than a gift.
